sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 142, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- Plan: ** On reset, systematically move green center to each object position and click corresponding container, checking for level completion after each
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 141 actions; a strong score needs about 59 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good! The green center CAN move:
- LEFT: moved from cols 12-13 to cols 10-11 ✓
- UP: moved from rows 38-39 to rows 36-37 ✓
Now the green center is at rows 36-37, cols 10-11. Looking at the ascii:
- Row 36: ccbbNNccccc (green center at cols 10-11, blue block at cols 8-9)
- Row 37: ccbbNNccccc
So the green center moved UP into the blue block's area! The blue block was at rows 34-37, cols 8-11, and now the green center is at rows 36-37, cols 10-11, which overlaps with the blue block.
Wait, looking at the ascii more carefully:
- Row 34: ccbbbbccccc (blue at cols 8-11)
- Row 35: ccbbbbccccc (blue at cols 8-11)
- Row 36: ccbbNNccccc (blue at cols 8-9, green at cols 10-11)
- Row 37: ccbbNNccccc (blue at cols 8-9, green at cols 10-11)
So the blue block is now at rows 34-37, cols 8-9 (narrower), and the green center is at rows 36-37, cols 10-11. The green center seems to have replaced part of the blue block.
This is interesting! The green center can move into the blue block's area, and the blue block shrinks to accommodate it.
Now I understand the game better:
1. The green center can move around the left side
2. The green center can overlap with other objects
3. Clicking containers toggles/swaps objects
But I still don't know the winning condition. I've used 141 actions, which is way over the 59 target.
Let me think about what I haven't tried:
1. Maybe the goal is to move the green center to a specific position and click a container
2. Maybe the goal is to have the green center overlap with a specific object when clicking a container
3. Maybe the goal is to achieve a specific configuration of objects
Looking at the current state:
- Green center at rows 36-37, cols 10-11 (overlapping with blue block)
- Red bar vertical at rows 24-29, cols 18-21
- Blue block at rows 34-37, cols 8-9 (partially overlapped by green center)
- Checkerboard at rows 20-23, cols 18-21
- Dark red box at rows 30-33, cols 18-21
Let me try clicking the blue container now that the green center is overlapping with the blue block. Maybe this produces a different effect.
Actually, I've been spending too many actions on exploration. Let me try a completely different approach: maybe the goal is simply to click the yellow square? The yellow square is at rows 20-21, cols 24-25, and I haven't interacted with it yet.
Let me try clicking the yellow square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Green center is at rows 36-37, cols 10-11 (overlapping blue block)
# Let me try clicking the yellow square - I haven't tried that yet!

# Yellow square is at rows 20-21, cols 24-25
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 20, 'col': 24}])
print("After clicking yellow square:")
print("  Board changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
print("  Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
print("  Done:", last_action_result.get('done'))
print("  Game over:", last_action_result.get('game_over'))

# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nObjects on left side:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'R', 'r', 'b', 'g', 'Y']:
        boundary = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in boundary]
        cols = [p[1] for p in boundary]
        center_col = sum(cols) // len(cols)
        if center_col < 32:
            print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
